does: This paper introduces MILD, a mediator agent system that repositions human drivers as active managers rather than passive supervisors in human-vehicle collaboration. The system architecture integrates a perception agent that monitors both in-cabin driver state and out-of-cabin environment, combined with a lightweight strategy agent that generates explainable action suggestions. The core contribution centers on Evidence- and Constraint-weighted Policy Optimization (ECPO), a novel method that ensures action suggestions comply with safety regulations and human values. The system addresses the fundamental problem of bidirectional misalignment: drivers lack transparency into vehicle decision logic while automated systems remain unaware of driver state and preferences. By providing interpretable recommendations and continuously monitoring driver attention and preferences, MILD aims to enhance shared situational awareness and reduce coordination failures in human-vehicle interaction.
connects: This work builds on extensive research showing that partial driving automation increases cognitive load through transparency failures and misaligned mental models between humans and machines. It extends prior work on explainable AI in autonomous vehicles and human-agent teaming, responding to documented coordination breakdowns in SAE Level 2 automation systems. The paper connects to the broader literature on shared autonomy and human-in-the-loop systems that seek to maintain driver engagement and situational awareness. It incorporates insights from human factors research on trust, transparency, and human-robot collaboration, while drawing on contemporary agentic AI systems architecture. The development of ECPO as a constrained optimization method for value alignment relates to recent advances in human value alignment and reinforcement learning from human feedback.
enables: This work enables future human-vehicle systems where explainability and adaptive alignment form the core rather than periphery of autonomous driving design. By demonstrating how to integrate driver state monitoring with real-time strategy suggestions, MILD sets up follow-on work in continuous human-vehicle negotiation and adaptive automation levels. The ECPO framework creates a foundation for future research in policy optimization under competing constraints from regulations, safety, and human preferences, applicable beyond driving to broader human-AI collaboration scenarios. The mediator architecture could extend to multi-agent scenarios, fleet coordination, or other domains requiring transparent human-machine partnerships. Long-term, this approach may inform standards for human-centered automation design that maintain driver agency and cognitive engagement rather than removing the human from control loops.
